text: Central-local government relations have undergone significant changes in Japan in recent years. This chapter positions the Japanese unitary system in historical and comparative perspective, arguing that Japanese local government is characterized by “interfusion” – being generally competent in the provision of individual public services, while being fully incorporated in the bureaucracy and closely regulated by the centre. This chapter tracks the interplay of the inter-fusion between administrative, fiscal and political levels over time, from the Meiji period to the post-war era, through to the decentralization reforms implemented in the 1990s. The evolution of central-local relations are illuminated through the examination of particular events in the history of Japan's administrative system, such as wartime functional centralization and the post-war reforms implemented under the Allied Occupation, which led to the formation of the present two-tier administrative structure. The chapter assesses the consequences of recent attempts at decentralization and draws conclusions as to their viability and future implications for central-local government relations in Japan, including particularly during and after the period of COVID-19, when all aspects of the administrative system have had to work in order to meet the numerous emergency needs arising from the pandemic. The chapter also examines the other longstanding structural problem affecting Japan's administration - the depopulation and aging of the local communities they must serve and be accountable to. Overall, the interfused structure of Japanese administration has proved sufficiently resilient and enduring for the principle of local government at the base of the administrative system to be retained.
